text: members of the social group; and (4) reintegration of the disturbed social group or social recognition of an irreparable breach or schism (Turner 1957a: 91–4). 1957: A Pivotal Year It is not so difficult to establish when Turner’s work took a decisively new direction: this happened in 1957, the same year as his thesis was published. Turner was one of the most promising young anthropologists of his generation. He was sponsored by the established elite, trained in the canon, and his dissertation was well-received. Had he gone down the road prepared for him, we would still today know him as an important member of the British school of anthropology. Luckily, he just could not go down that road. 1957 was the pivotal year, but Turner was of course ‘prepared’ for the rupture that ensued. The notion of ‘social drama’ had already opened the door for another way of approaching social life. From his mother, Turner had inherited a profound interest in the theatrical and creative side of life. He had a lifelong interest not only in ritual, but also in art, literature and poetry (which he also wrote). There is also no doubt that Turner was transformed by the ethnographic context in which he found himself. Among the Ndembu rituals were omnipresent, and social life largely organized around a variety of such rituals. In her memory of their fieldwork periods, Edith Turner (1985: 2–3) tells how the beating of the ritual drums could be heard so often that she and Victor were simply forced by circumstance to rethink the social role of rituals. Almost in the secret, 4 and against 4 As argued also in Deflem’s reconstruction, Turner’s hesitation with regard to immersing himself fully in the Ndembu ritual complex was surely related to his position within the Rhodes-Livingstone Institute of Sociological Research (Kuper 1983:128–9, 150–53; Deflem 1991). Studies of ritual had a very low priority at the Institute, which focused on political and legal systems, urbanization, labour migration, and social and economic organization. The Institute produced studies with a high degree of (neo-Marxist) uniformity whereby ‘deviants and turncoats were treated with great ferocity internally, but no criticism was tolerated from outsiders’ (Kuper 1983:129). Liminality and the Modern 78 the explicit recommendations of Max Gluckman (who ordered Turner to cover every aspect of ‘social structure’ before engaging with the ritual ‘superstructure’), Turner slowly came to consider ritual the core of his interest (for further detail, see Deflem’s excellent discussion of Turner’s intellectual development, 1991). The year 1957 is pivotal for the intellectual history of liminality, starting with the obvious but important observation that this was the year Arnold van Gennep died. Unsurprisingly, the Année Sociologique made no mention of the fact in its subsequent issues. The year 1958 was also the 100th anniversary of Durkheim’s birth, and the front cover of the 1958 issue bore his image, almost as a symbol of van Gennep’s non-existence. Van Gennep died as he had for many decades been living: silently and without public attention. He was mourned only by a small circle of folklorists who knew about his enormous project, and with whom he had been collaborating. However, as if by destiny, something else happened in 1957, and it concerned the life and thought of Victor Turner. Turner published Schism and Continuity, introducing the notion of social drama. But what is less known is that in 1957 Turner also wrote his first essay on ritual proper (Turner 1957b). With this paper, not published until 1964 (it was circulated among a smaller group of friends), Turner to a large extent laid the foundation of his approach to ritual and started to mark his difference to the conflict theory framework of the Manchester School. As always with Victor Turner, academic matters did not evolve in a vacuum: they concerned a worldview, and ethos, and also, as Weber would call it, a conduct of life. In 1957 Turner resigned from the British Communist Party and renounced Marxism, and he would never turn back on his decision. Deflem (1991) suggests that this categorical break might also have been related to the Soviet suppression of the Hungarian uprising, an event that stirred heated debates in all European left- wing environments. Later that year Turner was received into the Roman Catholic Church. He remained a Catholic for the rest of his life. During his years as Simon Research Fellow, lecturer and senior lecturer at Victoria University of Manchester (1957 to 1963), Turner started to devote his time almost entirely to writings on Ndembu ritual. It was not going to go down well with his surroundings. In 1963 Turner was offered a professorship of anthropology at Cornell University in Ithaca, New York. He had previously been appointed a fellow at the Center for Advanced Studies in the Behavioral Sciences at Palo Alto, California (1961–1962). He accepted the offer and decided to move to America. British academia had become a straightjacket to Turner, and the experimental tendencies in American science would attract him much more (and here again the parallels to Gregory Bateson are more than obvious). 1963: Turner Encounters Arnold van Gennep With the reorientation towards process and ritual, moving outside Durkheimian functionalism and neo-Marxism, Turner seemed destined to encounter the work of Arnold van Gennep, but the precise manner in which it happened still merits our Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 79 attention. Victor Turner, like all other Anglo-American anthropologists, did not really know about the work of Arnold van Gennep. During his fieldwork, Turner had read about van Gennep via the work of Henri Junod (Turner 1985: 159), so an indirect connection had been made. Luckily for the history of social thought, Solon Kimball had the felicitous intuition to translate Rites of Passage into English (otherwise we cannot be certain that Turner would actually have read it; he was not fluent in French). Turner stumbled upon van Gennep’s Rites of Passage almost by chance during the summer of 1963. Quite importantly, this happened at a moment
